But the farmer is not solely a
producer. He is also consumer.
He consumes shovels, forks, wag-
ons, plOws, reapers, harness and
the like. He goes to the polls and
votes for noncompetition on prices
of these things. He does not say.
I'VE are paying so cents for pitch
forks today" He pays an organ
ICED interest" price, because there
is no effective political cooperation
among farmers to put them on a
level with organized interests with
which they do business. For the
farmer, prices on both his products
and necessities are fixed by others.
Byt what others? BY pit gamblers
in New York and Chicago, by trans
portation monopolies and protected
manufacturing trusts-by organ.
lied interests"
